layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 4 Chapter 1 The Drude Theory of Metals

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Drude applied kinetic theory to this "gas" of conduction electrons of mass m, which

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: (in contrast to the molecules of an ordinary gas) move against a background of heavy

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: immobile ions. The density of the electron gas can be calculated as follows:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 5 This is the range for metallic elements under normal conditions. Higher densities can be attained

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: by application of pressure (which tends to favor the metallic state). Lower densities are found in com-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: pounds.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is the mass density (in grams per cubic centimeter)

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and A is the atomic mass of the element. Since each atom contributes Z electrons,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the number of electrons per cubic centimeter,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: both in angstroms

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cm) and in units of the Bohr radius

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: cm; the latter length, being a measure of the radius of a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: hydrogen atom in its ground state, is often used as a scale for measuring atomic

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: distances. Note that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is between 2 and 3 in most cases, although it ranges between

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 3 and 6 in the alkali metals (and can be as large as 10 in some metallic compounds).

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: These densities are typically a thousand times greater than those of a classical gas

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: at normal temperatures and pressures. In spite of this and in spite of the strong

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: electron-electron and electron-ion electromagnetic interactions, the Drude model

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: boldly treats the dense metallic electron gas by the methods of the kinetic theory of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a neutral dilute gas, with only slight modifications. The basic assumptions are these:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Strictly speaking, the electron-ion interaction is not entirely ignored, for the Drude model im-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plicitly assumes that the electrons are confined to the interior of the metal. Evidently this confinement is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: brought about by their attraction to the positively charged ions. Gross effects of the electron-ion and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: defn inteal fel epreentingtheveag effethe lectonlecton an econ-on ntecions.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Table 1.1 shows the conduction electron densities for some selected metals,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: They are typically of order

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: conduction electrons per cubic centimeter, varying

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: from

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for cesium up to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: for beryllium. Also listed in

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Table 1.1 is a widely used measure of the electronic density.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: defined as the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: radius of a sphere whose volume is equal to the volume per conduction electron.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thus

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 1. Between collisions the interaction of a given electron, both with the others

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and with the ions, is neglected. Thus in the absence of externally applied electro-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: magnetic fields each electron is taken to move uniformly in a straight line. In the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: presence of externally applied fields each electron is taken to move as determined

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: by Newton's laws of motion in the presence of those external fields, but neglecting

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the additional complicated fields produced by the other electrons and ions.6 The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: neglect of electron-electron interactions between collisions is known as the indepen-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dent electron approximation. The corresponding neglect of electron-ion interactions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is known as the free electron approximation. We shall find in subsequent chapters that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Basic Assumptions of the Drude Model

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: although the independent electron approximation is in many contexts surprisingly

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: good, the free electron approximation must be abandoned if one is to arrive at even

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a qualitative understanding of much of metallic behavior.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 2. Collisions in the Drude model, as in kinetic theory, are instantaneous events

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: that abruptly alter the velocity of an electron. Drude attributed them to the electrons

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: bouncing off the impenetrable ion cores (rather than to electron-electron collisions.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the analogue of the predominant collision mechanism in an ordinary gas). We shall

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: find later that electron-electron scattering is indeed one of the least important of the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: several scattering mechanisms in a metal, except under unusual conditions. However,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Crystal Structures, 2nd ed., Interscience, New York, 1963.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 6 Chapter 1 The Drude Theory of Metals

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: According to Ohm's law, the current I flowing in a wire is proportional to the potential

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: drop V along the wire: V = IR, where R, the resistance of the wire, depends on its

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: For some time people were led into diffcult but irrelevant problems connected with the proper

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: aig o n electron  in eac colion.S iteral n iteretation o Figure 1. is eu

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to be avoided.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Given the free and independent electron approximation, this is the only possible mechanism left.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: In the rest of this chapter we shall illustrate these notions through their most

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: important applications, noting the extent to which they succeed or fail to describe

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the observed phenomena.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the simple mechanical picture (Figure 1.2) of an electron bumping along from ion to

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ion is very far off the mark.7 Fortunately, this does not matter for many purposes:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: a qualitative (and often a quantitative) understanding of metallic conduction can

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: be achieved by simply assuming that there is some scattering mechanism, without

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: inquiring too closely into just what that mechanism might be. By appealing, in our

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: analysis. to only a few general effects of the collision process. we can avoid committing

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ourselves to any specific picture of how electron scattering actually takes place. These

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: broad features are described in the following two assumptions.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Figure 1.2

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Trajectory of a conduction electron scattering off the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ions. according to the naive picture of Drude.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 3. We shall assume that an electron experiences a collision (i.e. suffers an abrupt

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: change in its velocity) with a probability per unit time 1/τ. We mean by this that the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: probability of an electron undergoing a collision in any infinitesimal time interval of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: length dt is just dt/t. The time t is variously known as the relaxation time, the collision

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: time, or the mean free time, and it plays a fundamental role in the theory of metallic

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: conduction. It follows from this assumption that an electron picked at random at a

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: given moment will, on the average, travel for a time t before its next collision, and

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: will, on the average, have been traveling for a time t since its last collision.8 In the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: simplest applications of the Drude model the collision time t is taken to be inde-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: pendent of an electron's position and velocity. We shall see later that this turns out

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to be a surprisingly good assumption for many (but by no means all) applications.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: 4. Electrons are assumed to achieve thermal equilibrium with their surroundings

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: only through collisions. These collisions are assumed to maintain local thermo-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: dynamic equilibrium in a particularly simple way: immediately after each collision

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: an electron is taken to emerge with a velocity that is not related to its velocity

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: just before the collision, but randomly directed and with a speed appropriate to the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: temperature prevailing at the place where the collision occurred. Thus the hotter the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: region in which a collision occurs, the faster a typical electron will emerge from the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: collision.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: DC ELECTRICAL CONDUCTIVITY OF A METAL

layout_dets.text: dimensions, but is independent of the size of the current or potential drop. The Drude

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: model accounts for this behavior and provides an estimate of the size of the resistance.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: This result is usually stated in terms of the inverse of the resistivity, the conductivity

layout_dets.text: One generally eliminates the dependence of R on the shape of the wire by intro-

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: ducing a quantity characteristic only of the metal of which the wire is composed. The

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: resistivity

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: is defined to be the proportionality constant between the electric field

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: E at a point in the metal and the current density j that it induces10.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: The current density j is a vector. parallel to the flow of charge, whose magnitude is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the amount of charge per unit time crossing a unit area perpendicular to the flow.

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Thus if a uniform current I fows through a wire of length L and cross-sectional area

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: A, the current density will be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Since the potential drop along the wire will be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: , Eq. (1.3) gives

layout_dets.label: ocr_text

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: If n electrons per unit volume all move with velocity v, then the current density

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: they give rise to will be parallel to v. Furthermore, in a time dt the electrons will

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: advance by a distance v dt in the direction of v, so that

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: electrons will cross

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: an area A perpendicular to the direction of flow. Since each electron carries a charge

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: the charge crossing 4 in the time dt will be

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and hence the current

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: density is

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: At any point in a metal, electrons are always moving in a variety of directions

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: with a variety of thermal energies. The net current density is thus given by (1.4),

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: where v is the average electronic velocity. In the absence of an electric field, electrons

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: are as likely to be moving in any one direction as in any other, v averages to zero,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: and, as expected, there is no net electric current density. In the presence of a field E,

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: however, there will be a mean electronic velocity directed opposite to the feld (the

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: electronic charge being negative), which we can compute as follows:

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: Consider a typical electron at time zero. Let t be the time elapsed since its last

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: collision. Its velocity at time zero will be its velocity

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: immediately after that collision

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: plus the additional velocity

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: it has subsequently acquired. Since we assume

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: that an electron emerges from a collision in a random direction, there will be no

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: contribution from

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: to the average electronic velocity, which must therefore be given

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: entirely by the average of

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: However, the average of t is the relaxation time

layout_dets.label: ocr_text
layout_dets.text: . Therefore
